520__a-1: This is an architectural blueprint on aged, beige paper showing detailed construction drawings of a Victorian-era building. The main central element is a large longitudinal section view displaying the building's interior profile from left to right, featuring a prominent tall tower with a pointed spire on the left side, multiple peaked roof structures, various interior levels with indicated heights, windows, doors, staircases, and architectural details rendered in pen and ink. The building appears to be several stories tall with ornamental railings and balconies visible. Above the main section is a smaller cross-sectional elevation labeled "Staircase Section" showing the building's depth and roof construction details. At the bottom, a ground floor plan or additional section view is indicated. Various handwritten annotations and measurements are scattered throughout the drawing, including dimension lines and architectural notes. The text appears to include labels such as "Longitudinal Section" (visible at the bottom), though many smaller notations are difficult to read clearly due to the image resolution and age of the document. A color reference bar appears on the right side of the drawing. The overall composition demonstrates professional architectural documentation typical of 19th-century building design.

# Architectural Blueprint Description

This image shows a detailed architectural floor plan on aged paper, titled "SECOND FLOOR" at the bottom. At the top of the page is a color reference bar with numbered sections in dark blue, cyan, green, yellow, magenta, pink, white, and dark gray, along with a measurement ruler. The plan itself features two main levels rendered in brown ink lines on a cream-colored background with visible age stains and deterioration marks distributed across the surface.

The layout displays multiple rooms labeled as "Bed Room" (marked with numbers 3 and 4), "Bathroom," "Hall," "Staircase," and other residential spaces with their dimensions noted in feet and inches. Doorways, windows, and architectural details are indicated with standard architectural symbols. The upper portion shows larger rooms with diagonal line patterns, while the lower level presents a more complex arrangement of smaller rooms arranged in a linear fashion. Text at the bottom identifies this as being drawn by "F. MCINDOE & SONS, ARCHTS." with an address of "530 PINE ST. S.F." The scale notation reads "SCALED EIGHT FEET TO ONE INCH." The document shows significant wear consistent with a historical architectural drawing, with brown staining and creased edges along the borders.

# Architectural Floor Plan Description

This is a historical architectural blueprint showing the first floor layout of a residential building, drawn to a scale of eight feet to one inch. The document is aged parchment with visible staining and wear marks throughout. At the top of the page is a color reference bar displaying navy blue, cyan, green, yellow, magenta, white, and dark gray swatches, along with a measurement ruler.

The floor plan depicts a multi-section residence with distinct areas arranged vertically and horizontally. The upper portion contains service areas including a kitchen (labeled 13'-6"x9'), pantry, and various closets. The central section features two symmetrical dining rooms labeled "Old Ladies' Dining Room" (30'-30'6") and "Children's Dining Room" (30'-30'6"), with a central circulation space between them. The middle area includes parlors, a vestibule, and children's entrance hall.

The lower section shows the basement or ground floor layout with multiple bedrooms, halls, sitting rooms, and storage areas. Room dimensions are marked throughout in feet and inches. Architectural elements including doorways, windows, stairs, and fireplaces are indicated with standard symbols and notations.

At the bottom, the credit reads "E. M. MOOSGALL & SON, ARCHTS, 330 PINE STR., S.F." with the notation "FIRST FLOOR" beneath the scale information. The plan is rendered in light brown ink on aged cream-colored paper.
